entry.title: Combined summary - Regular signet reorgs
entry.summary: The recent enhancements in the signet miner code have introduced a systematic approach for initiating blockchain reorganizations (reorgs) using a bash script. This script determines the timing of reorgs through a cryptographic process involving SHA-256 hashing and a modulo operation, which leads to reorgs being scheduled based on specific hash criteria. The new method was evidenced by a reorganization at [block 307073](https://mempool.space/signet/block/00000006e627e02fe27eec8dbf1fc2a83839c8d92475ba807c99d3a118f035b4), resulting in a two-block reorg. This update not only showcases the operational capabilities of the script but also raises questions about the anticipated duration and frequency of such reorgs, given their dependency on mining times and a hardcoded secret value used within the hashing process. Additionally, the signet network has been restored and is observable via [fork.observer](https://fork.observer/?network=3). Recent observations revealed a two-block reorganization at the specified block height, identifying different miners' contributions through coinbase tags, with 'signet-3 (inquisition)' associated with one's mining activities and 'signet-1' connected to an individual named Kalle. These insights are crucial for analyzing participant dynamics within the blockchain network. There is also a mention of a backup mechanism termed "signet-2," aimed at ensuring continuity in block creation if primary systems fail. This reflects a robust infrastructure designed to maintain functionality amidst system disruptions. Furthermore, the communication detailed technical specifics of two blockchain forks, noting their block heights, hash values, branch lengths, and statuses as valid forks, which are vital for tracking developments within blockchain technology. The strategy for triggering reorgs has been refined to occur only if the previous block is aged 25 minutes, enhancing the likelihood that a reorg will effectively integrate into the blockchain as the more-work chain. Should this integration fail initially, the system is designed to reassess and potentially rejoin consensus with the original block and its descendants shortly thereafter. However, these reorgs might be causing transactions to be ejected from my mempool but not from others, affecting transaction processing until they are manually re-submitted. This issue also seems to impact fee rate estimation, as documented in [bitcoin35507](https://github.com/bitcoin/bitcoin/issues/35507), and could influence transactions in active wallets on my mining nodes.
